---
title: "Correlation vs causation"
module: "M2 — Math You Actually Need"
chapter: 13
difficulty: "beginner"
prereqs:
  - "M2 Ch11 — Mean, variance & standard deviation"
learning_objectives:
  - "Read a correlation coefficient (−1 to +1) as the strength and direction of a linear relationship"
  - "Explain why correlation does not prove causation"
  - "Spot a confounding variable behind a misleading correlation"
compute: "browser"
status: "stable"
last_reviewed: "2026-06-29"
est_minutes: 14
resources:
  - ../../lib/grader.py
review_cards:
  - id: correlation-range
    q: "What range does a correlation coefficient take, and what do its extremes mean?"
    a: "From −1 to +1. +1 is a perfect rise-together line, −1 is a perfect opposite line, and 0 means no linear relationship. It measures linear co-movement, not cause."
  - id: correlation-not-causation
    q: "Why does a strong correlation not prove that one thing causes the other?"
    a: "A correlation only shows that two variables move together. The link could run the other way (reverse causation) or be driven by a third variable. Causation needs an experiment or careful control of other factors."
  - id: what-is-a-confounder
    q: "What is a confounding variable? Give the ice-cream example."
    a: "A confounder is a hidden third variable that drives two others, making them look linked. Ice-cream sales and drowning deaths rise together because summer heat drives both — heat is the confounder, not a cause running between them."
format:
  live-html:
    theme:
      light:
        - cosmo
        - ../../theme/larnix.scss
      dark:
        - darkly
        - ../../theme/larnix-dark.scss
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    include-after-body: ../../theme/_after-body.html
execute:
  enabled: false
---

{{< badge difficulty=beginner >}} {{< badge compute=browser >}} {{< badge status=stable >}}

## The hook

Every summer, two numbers climb together: **ice-cream sales** and **drowning
deaths**. Track them month by month and the pattern is unmistakable — when one
goes up, so does the other. So should we ban ice cream to save lives?

Of course not. Ice cream doesn't drown anyone. **Summer heat** drives both: hot
weather sells more ice cream *and* sends more people swimming, where more
drownings happen. The heat is hiding behind the scenes, pulling both strings. It
is a **confounder** — a third variable that makes two unrelated things look
linked.

This chapter is about telling those two situations apart: when numbers *move
together* (correlation) versus when one number *makes the other happen*
(causation). Mixing them up is one of the most common — and most expensive —
mistakes in data work.

> You'll need from before: the mean and how spread is measured (M2 Ch11). We
> reuse the idea of "distance from the mean" to build correlation.

---

## Correlation: a number from −1 to +1

**Correlation** turns "these two things move together" into a single number, the
correlation coefficient, usually written **r**. It always lands between −1 and
+1:

- **r = +1** — a *perfect positive* relationship. The points sit exactly on a
  line that rises to the right: when one variable goes up, the other goes up by a
  proportional amount, every time.
- **r = −1** — a *perfect negative* relationship. The points sit on a line that
  falls to the right: when one goes up, the other goes down.
- **r = 0** — *no linear* relationship. Knowing one variable tells you nothing
  about whether the other is high or low.

Values in between measure how *tight* and which *direction* the trend is. An
r of 0.9 is a strong rise-together pattern with a little scatter; an r of 0.2 is a
weak, noisy one; an r of −0.6 is a moderately strong downward trend.

Two cautions worth keeping in mind:

- Correlation measures **linear** co-movement only. A relationship can be strong
  but curved (think of a U-shape) and still give an r near 0, because it isn't a
  straight line.
- Correlation has **no units** and says nothing about steepness. An r of 1.0 does
  not mean "one-for-one"; it means the points fall perfectly on *some* straight
  line, however steep or shallow.

### Seeing it: hours studied vs exam score

Here is a small, made-up class: hours each student studied and the score they got.
The points drift up to the right — more study, higher score — so we expect a
positive r close to 1. The plot draws the scatter and prints the r that goes with
it, connecting the picture to the number.

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "More study hours line up with higher scores — a positive correlation"
#| fig-alt: "Scatter plot with study hours on the x-axis and exam score on the y-axis. Eight points rise from the lower-left to the upper-right in a roughly straight upward band, showing a strong positive trend."
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

hours = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8]
score = [52, 55, 60, 64, 69, 72, 79, 84]

r = np.corrcoef(hours, score)[0, 1]
print("correlation r =", round(r, 4))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 3))
ax.scatter(hours, score)
ax.set_xlabel("hours studied")
ax.set_ylabel("exam score")
ax.set_title(f"Study hours vs score (r = {round(r, 2)})")
plt.show()
```

The printed r is about **0.99** — a strong positive correlation, which matches the
tidy upward band your eye sees. (The canvas above has no alt text for a screen
reader, so the takeaway in words: the points climb steadily from lower-left to
upper-right, and the number confirms a near-perfect upward line.)

But notice what the number *can't* tell you. A strong r here is consistent with
"studying raises scores" — and that's plausible — but the number alone doesn't
prove it. That's the whole point of the next section.

---

## Causation: a higher bar

To say A **causes** B is to claim that *changing* A would *change* B. Correlation
never establishes that on its own, because at least three different stories can
produce the same rise-together pattern:

1. **A really does cause B.** Studying more does raise scores.
2. **Reverse causation — B causes A.** Maybe the relationship runs the other way
   from what you assumed. (Do people exercise because they're healthy, or are they
   healthy because they exercise? The correlation looks the same either way.)
3. **A confounder causes both.** A hidden third variable drives A and B together,
   so they correlate without either causing the other — the ice-cream-and-drowning
   case, with summer heat behind both.

Because all three produce the same correlation, a number alone can't pick the
winner. To earn the word *causation*, you need more:

- A **controlled experiment** — change A on purpose for a randomly chosen group,
  hold everything else as steady as you can, and see if B changes. Randomising
  breaks the link to confounders, which is why randomised trials are the gold
  standard.
- Failing that, **statistically controlling** for the variables you suspect — for
  example, comparing ice-cream sales and drownings *within* days of the same
  temperature, so heat can't be doing the work.

The headline to carry away: a correlation is a *clue*, not a *verdict*. It tells
you where to look, not what's true.

---

## Spotting the confounder

The practical skill is asking, whenever two things correlate: **"What else could
be driving both?"** A confounder is a variable that sits upstream of both
measurements and creates the link.

- Ice-cream sales ↔ drownings — confounder: **summer heat**.
- Shoe size ↔ reading ability in children — confounder: **age** (older kids have
  bigger feet *and* read better).
- Number of firefighters at a blaze ↔ amount of damage — confounder: **the size
  of the fire** (bigger fires draw more crews *and* cause more damage).

In every case, acting on the raw correlation — banning ice cream, handing out big
shoes, sending fewer firefighters — would fail, because you'd be tugging on
something that was never the cause. Naming the confounder is what turns a
misleading number into an understood one.

---

## Key Takeaways

::: {.key-takeaways}
### Key Takeaways

1. **Correlation** is a single number, r, from **−1 to +1**: +1 perfect
   rise-together, −1 perfect opposite, 0 no linear link.
2. Correlation measures **linear co-movement** only — a strong curved
   relationship can still show r near 0.
3. **Correlation is not causation.** The same correlation can come from A→B, B→A
   (reverse causation), or a confounder driving both.
4. To claim causation you need an **experiment** or careful **control** of other
   variables — a high r on its own is not enough.
5. When two things correlate, ask **"what else could drive both?"** — that hidden
   third variable is the **confounder**.
:::

---

## Practice

Each exercise is auto-graded by `run_tests`, loaded for you from `lib/grader.py`.

### Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢

NumPy already knows how to compute a correlation coefficient. Complete
`correlation(x, y)` so it returns the r between two lists, using
`np.corrcoef(x, y)`, which gives a 2×2 matrix whose off-diagonal entry `[0, 1]` is
the r you want. Replace `____`, then press **Run**.

```{pyodide}
#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_corr
from lib.grader import run_tests
```

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: ex_corr
import numpy as np

def correlation(x, y):
    """Return the correlation coefficient r between two lists of numbers."""
    return ____  # TODO: the [0, 1] entry of np.corrcoef(x, y)

run_tests([
    ("perfect positive", round(correlation([1, 2, 3, 4], [2, 4, 6, 8]), 4), 1.0),
    ("perfect negative", round(correlation([1, 2, 3, 4], [8, 6, 4, 2]), 4), -1.0),
])
```

<details><summary>If you're stuck</summary>

`np.corrcoef(x, y)` returns a 2×2 matrix. The diagonal is each variable's
correlation with itself (always 1). The number you want — x with y — is the
off-diagonal entry, `np.corrcoef(x, y)[0, 1]`.
</details>

<details><summary>Show solution</summary>

```python
import numpy as np

def correlation(x, y):
    return np.corrcoef(x, y)[0, 1]
```

`[2, 4, 6, 8]` is exactly `2 *` the x values, so the points sit on a perfect rising
line and r is +1. `[8, 6, 4, 2]` falls as x rises, a perfect line the other way, so
r is −1.
</details>

### Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡

Correlation is built on a simpler quantity: **covariance**, the average of "how far
x is from its mean" times "how far y is from its mean". When x and y tend to be
above (or below) their means together, the products are positive and covariance is
positive; when one is high while the other is low, the products are negative.

Write `covariance(x, y)` for the **population** covariance:
`mean of (x − mean(x)) * (y − mean(y))`. Complete the marked line.

```{pyodide}
#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_cov
from lib.grader import run_tests
```

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: ex_cov
import numpy as np

def covariance(x, y):
    """Population covariance: the mean of the product of the two deviations."""
    x = np.array(x)
    y = np.array(y)
    return ____  # TODO: mean of (x - mean(x)) * (y - mean(y))

run_tests([
    ("rises together", round(covariance([1, 2, 3, 4], [1, 2, 3, 4]), 4), 1.25),
    ("moves opposite", round(covariance([1, 2, 3, 4], [4, 3, 2, 1]), 4), -1.25),
])
```

<details><summary>If you're stuck</summary>

Build it in pieces: `np.mean(x)` is the mean of x. The deviations are
`x - np.mean(x)` and `y - np.mean(y)`. Multiply the two deviation arrays
element-wise, then take `np.mean(...)` of the result.
</details>

<details><summary>Show solution</summary>

```python
import numpy as np

def covariance(x, y):
    x = np.array(x)
    y = np.array(y)
    return np.mean((x - np.mean(x)) * (y - np.mean(y)))
```

For `[1, 2, 3, 4]` the mean is 2.5, so the deviations are −1.5, −0.5, 0.5, 1.5.
Multiplied by themselves they give 2.25, 0.25, 0.25, 2.25, which sum to 5; divided
by 4 values that's 1.25. When the second list runs the other way, every product
flips sign, giving −1.25.
</details>

### Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴

Open-ended, **rubric-graded** (not auto-checked). Name a pair of things from
everyday life that you'd expect to correlate, state the **confounder** you think is
really behind both, and explain why acting on the correlation as if it were causal
would backfire.

**Rubric — a good answer:**

- Names a believable correlated pair (two things that really do move together).
- Identifies a plausible **confounding variable** that drives both.
- Explains the failure: what action someone might take on the raw correlation, and
  why it wouldn't get the result they want because the real cause is the confounder.

<details><summary>Show one possible answer</summary>

A classic pair: **cities with more parks** tend to have **healthier residents**,
so a city council might think "build parks → healthier people" and pour the budget
into parkland alone.

The likely confounder is **household wealth**. Wealthier neighbourhoods tend to
have both more green space *and* residents who can afford good food, healthcare,
and leisure time to exercise. Wealth drives both the parks and the health.

Acting on the raw correlation could backfire: dropping parks into a struggling
neighbourhood without addressing income, food access, or healthcare may produce
little health gain, because the parks were a *marker* of wealth, not the *cause* of
the health. The fix is to compare neighbourhoods of similar wealth, or run a trial,
before concluding parks themselves do the work.
</details>

---

## Check yourself

{{< quiz quiz-ch13.yml >}}

---

## Where this fits / what's next

You can now read an r as strength-and-direction, and you know its limit: a
correlation is a clue about a relationship, never proof that one thing causes
another. That caution is the backbone of honest data analysis — and it sets up the
modelling work ahead, where we *fit* lines to data and have to keep asking what the
numbers really mean. Next we turn correlation into a line you can draw and use.
